Glossary About
Machine Minds

Walter Pitts

1923 – 1969

Logical neurons and the computational abstraction of nervous activity.

Walter Pitts did not leave a systematic philosophy of mind in the way that Aristotle, Descartes, Brentano, Husserl, Ryle, or Chalmers did. The most responsible way to proceed is therefore reconstructive: to extract the philosophical commitments implicit in Pitts’s technical work, especially the 1943 McCulloch–Pitts paper, Pitts’s 1943 solo work on non-symbolic learning and conditioning, the 1947 Pitts–McCulloch paper on universals, and the 1959 frog-vision paper with Lettvin, Maturana, McCulloch, and Pitts. The result is not “Pitts’s doctrine of the soul” in a confessional or metaphysical sense, but a rigorous neurocomputational metaphysics of mind that bears directly on older doctrines of soul, intellect, universals, perception, selfhood, and finality.

My central thesis is this: Pitts’s implied philosophy of mind is a neuro-immanent, computational, anti-Cartesian, anti-homuncular, and naturalized-transcendental theory of mental life. Mental events are not ghostly happenings added to matter; they are realized in organized nervous activity. Yet this is not crude behaviorism. Pitts’s work gives a formal account of intentionality, abstraction, perception of universals, learning, memory, purposiveness, and the limits of self-knowledge. The theological importance is real: Pitts’s framework displaces the “soul” as an explanatory inner agent for ordinary cognition, but it does not by itself refute every theological conception of soul. It is hostile to a Cartesian soul-as-inner-mechanism; it is more compatible with an Aristotelian or Thomistic reading of soul as the formal organization of a living body, though even there it would force major revisions.

1. Source and attribution problem

The first caution is that the most philosophical texts are co-authored, above all with Warren McCulloch. In the 1943 paper, McCulloch and Pitts present a logical calculus of nervous activity; in 1947, Pitts and McCulloch explicitly address the perception of universals; in 1959, Pitts participates in the empirical and theoretical shift represented by “What the Frog’s Eye Tells the Frog’s Brain.” The table of contents of McCulloch’s Embodiments of Mind also makes clear how closely these co-authored papers sit beside McCulloch’s explicitly philosophical and theological essays, including “Through the Den of the Metaphysician,” “Finality and Form,” “Mysterium Iniquitatis of Sinful Man Aspiring into the Place of God,” and “Toward Some Circuitry of Ethical Robots.” Those latter titles are McCulloch’s, not Pitts’s; they should not be attributed to Pitts. But they do show that the intellectual environment of Pitts’s work was not merely technical or engineering-oriented. It was openly metaphysical, epistemological, and, at its edges, theological.

The key primary text is “A Logical Calculus of the Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity” from 1943. Its title alone is philosophically charged. “Ideas” are not transcendent forms floating above the body; they are “immanent” in nervous activity. The paper argues that because nervous activity has an all-or-none character, neural events and their relations can be treated by propositional logic; every neural net can, in the authors’ model, be described in those terms, and for suitable logical expressions one can construct a net behaving accordingly.

That said, Pitts’s own solo work matters because it prevents a too-simple reading of him as a purely symbolic logician. In 1942, he published on simple neuron circuits; in 1943, he published a two-part mathematical theory of non-symbolic learning and conditioning, including reward, punishment, transfer, generalization, and discrimination. In other words, Pitts’s world was not just Boolean propositions; it also included conditioning, learning curves, reward, psychophysical relations, and network dynamics.

2. The core doctrine: ideas are neural, but neural activity is formal

The 1943 doctrine can be summarized in one sentence: mental contents are realized in nervous nets whose activity can be formally represented as logical structure. This is not merely the claim that the brain causes the mind. It is the stronger claim that the formal organization of mental life is present in, and recoverable from, the formal organization of neural events.

McCulloch and Pitts make five simplifying assumptions: neurons fire all-or-none; a fixed number of synapses must be excited within a time period to excite a neuron; synaptic delay is the only significant delay; inhibitory synapses prevent excitation; and the net’s structure does not change over time. These are deliberate idealizations, and they matter philosophically because the theory’s “mind” is initially a mind of stable, discrete, temporally ordered neural propositions.

But they also explicitly warn against mistaking formal equivalence for physiological explanation. They say that neither author regards formal equivalence as factual explanation; facilitation, extinction, and learning depend on continuous electrochemical changes and enduring changes that can survive sleep, anesthesia, convulsions, and coma. This is crucial. Pitts’s thought is computational, but not naïvely digital in the vulgar sense. The formal calculus abstracts from physiology; it does not abolish physiology.

The theory therefore has two levels. At the formal level, neural events can be mapped to propositions. At the biological level, real nervous systems involve thresholds, ionic changes, plasticity, learning, and anatomical constraints. A Pittsian philosophy of mind must keep both levels: mind is formally intelligible, but formal intelligibility is not identical with complete biological explanation.

3. Mind without a ghost: the anti-Cartesian consequence

The most direct metaphysical statement in the 1943 paper comes near the end. McCulloch and Pitts say that every idea and every sensation is realized by activity within the net, and they add that psychic units, or “psychons,” can be no less than the activity of single neurons. They then infer that psychic events are inherently propositional, intentional, or semiotic, and that the fundamental relations of psychology are those of two-valued propositional logic.

The theological and anti-dualist force of this is obvious. A “thought” is not an immaterial spark added to neural matter. A “sensation” is not an event occurring in a private spiritual theater. A “psychon” is not a mental atom in a soul-substance. For Pitts, at least in this reconstruction, a mental event is an organized neural event considered under its formal, propositional, and semiotic aspect.

The paper’s famous anti-ghostly conclusion follows: in such systems, “Mind” no longer becomes more ghostly than a ghost; diseased mentality can be understood scientifically in neurophysiological terms. This is not just an early computational slogan. It is a metaphysical claim: mental disorder, perception, memory, and purposive action are not disruptions or activities of a separate soul-like substance; they are disturbances or organizations of nervous activity.

This does not mean Pitts has an eliminative materialism in the later philosophical sense. He does not say that beliefs, sensations, and perceptions do not exist. He says they exist as activities of nets. His position is closer to a formal neuro-realism: mental phenomena are real, but their reality is immanent in the structure and activity of nervous systems.

4. Intentionality: all psychic events are semiotic

One of the most philosophically important lines in the 1943 paper is that all psychic events have an intentional or semiotic character. This is remarkable because intentionality had been the hallmark of mind since Brentano: mental states are “about” something. McCulloch and Pitts do not deny aboutness; they try to naturalize it. Neural events become proposition-like because their occurrence says, in effect, that certain conditions obtain within the net’s causal and sensory history.

The obvious difficulty is semantic: why should a neural firing count as a proposition rather than merely as a physical pulse? Piccinini’s close historical analysis identifies exactly this as one of the two major problematic moves in the 1943 theory: McCulloch and Pitts idealized neurons so that propositional inferences could be mapped to neural events, and they assumed that individual neural pulses had propositional contents directly explaining mental processes. Piccinini argues that neither move is widely acceptable today in its original form, even though the broader idea that neural computation explains mental activity remains influential.

A charitable reconstruction is that Pitts is not giving a full theory of semantic content. He is offering a formal criterion for intentionality: an event is mental insofar as it is embedded in a system of possible inferences, discriminations, and consequences. Aboutness is not a mysterious inner glow; it is the role an activity plays in a structured network relating afferent inputs, internal states, and efferent outputs. This is a precursor to later functionalist, causal, and informational theories of mental content.

5. Consciousness: present, but not isolated as a “hard problem”

Pitts does not offer a separate theory of phenomenal consciousness. There is no developed account of qualia, first-person givenness, subjectivity, or “what-it-is-like” experience. But consciousness is not absent. It appears indirectly through “sensation,” “sense awareness,” “perception,” “psychic events,” memory, and the continuum of experience.

The 1943 paper claims that its calculus gives constructional solutions to holistic problems involving the differentiated continuum of sense awareness and the normative, perfective, and resolvent properties of perception and execution. In other words, one of the intended philosophical payoffs is to show how continuous, structured awareness can arise from discrete all-or-none events.

A Pittsian theory of consciousness would therefore be neither Cartesian introspectionism nor later eliminativism. It would treat consciousness as an organized, temporally extended, recurrent, discriminative activity of a nervous net. The basic units are binary, but the experienced field is not a single binary unit; it is a constructed continuum generated by complex relations among neural activities.

The weakness is equally clear. Pitts gives a formal account of the structure of awareness, not a phenomenology of awareness. He can explain how a system discriminates, remembers, predicts, abstracts, and behaves purposively. He does not explain why those operations should feel like something. If one asks whether Pitts solves the “hard problem,” the answer is no. If one asks whether Pitts gives a mathematically precise theory of the functional architecture of awareness, the answer is yes, at least in embryo.

6. Time, memory, and the asymmetry of self-knowledge

Recurrent circuits are central to Pitts’s philosophy of mind. McCulloch and Pitts write that circular paths in the nervous system regenerate excitation, making reference to the past indefinite; recursive functions are required to specify the implications of such circuits.

This gives a striking theory of memory. Memory is not a stored image in a mental container. It is a present neural activity whose recurrence and structure imply a class of possible past afferent histories. The past is not directly visible from the present; it is underdetermined by the present state. That underdetermination is not an accident but a formal consequence of the net’s causal organization.

This produces one of the deepest epistemological claims in the paper. Given a present state and a specified net, one can compute the succeeding state; but one cannot uniquely infer the preceding state. Knowledge of the world, including ourselves, is incomplete in space and indefinite in time. The ignorance built into our brains is the counterpart of the abstraction that makes knowledge useful.

That is a profound philosophy of self-knowledge. The self is not transparent to itself. Introspection cannot reveal the full causes of its own present state. A memory is not a direct possession of the past but a present reconstruction constrained by a net. This anticipates later themes in cognitive science: memory is reconstructive, self-knowledge is partial, and first-person certainty is limited by subpersonal mechanisms.

7. A naturalized Kantianism: the net as condition of possible experience

The 1943 paper also makes a startling Kantian-sounding claim. If the net is altered, observations and facts alter; hallucinations, delusions, tinnitus, confusions, and disorientations show that if our nets are undefined, our facts are undefined. With determination of the net, they write, the “thing in itself” ceases to be unknowable.

This should not be read as crude idealism. Pitts is not saying that the world is created by the brain. He is saying that facts-as-known are constituted through the structure of the nervous net. The world’s afferent causes are not fully determined by central activity; the theory explicitly preserves that asymmetry. But the form in which the world appears as knowable fact depends on the net.

This is a naturalized transcendental philosophy. Kant asked: what are the conditions of possible experience? Pitts’s answer is: the conditions are the formal and physiological organization of neural nets. The a priori is not a disembodied structure of pure reason; it is a nervous architecture. The frog-vision paper later uses almost exactly this kind of language, describing genetically built-in retinal operations as a “physiological synthetic a priori.”

The result is a philosophy of mind in which epistemology becomes neurophysiology without becoming mere physiology. We know through forms imposed by neural organization. To know the mind is to know the structure by which the world becomes available as fact, object, form, continuity, memory, and action.

8. Universals: abstraction without an immaterial intellect

The 1947 Pitts–McCulloch paper, “How We Know Universals: The Perception of Auditory and Visual Forms,” is indispensable for any discussion of soul, mind, and consciousness because universals are one of the classical arguments for a non-sensory or spiritual intellect. If the mind can know “triangularity,” “same shape,” “same chord,” or “same form” across varying sensory instances, perhaps the mind must be more than a sensory mechanism.

Pitts and McCulloch answer by mechanizing the perception of universals. The paper describes two neural mechanisms for recognition of forms, both robust against small perturbations in excitation, threshold, and synchrony. One mechanism averages an apparition over a group; another reduces an apparition to a standard selected from among legitimate presentations. The examples are philosophically explicit: recognition of chords regardless of pitch and shapes regardless of size.

This is a naturalized theory of universals. A universal is not first of all a Platonic object or an immaterial concept. It is an invariant under transformations, captured by neural operations that preserve form across variations. To know a universal is to possess a mechanism that treats many presentations as equivalent under a relevant transformation.

The theological significance is substantial. In scholastic and Platonic traditions, abstraction from sensory particulars often supports a doctrine of intellect that exceeds the body. Pitts does not refute such doctrines globally, but he narrows their explanatory domain. At least for auditory and visual forms, the capacity to recognize universals can be modeled as a neural operation. A theological account of intellect can no longer simply point to ordinary perceptual abstraction as proof that the soul must operate independently of the nervous system.

9. Perception is not passive sensation: the frog’s eye as philosophical correction

The 1959 frog paper changes the tone of the philosophy. It is no longer enough to say that the brain receives sensory inputs and computes. The retina itself is already performing complex operations. The paper reports that frog optic nerve fibers respond not primarily to light intensity but to local patterns of variation; four types of fibers form distinct distributed channels conveying local pattern independent of average illumination.

The behavioral framing is equally important. The frog does not appear concerned with stationary detail; it may starve amid unmoving food, while leaping at small moving objects of the right kind. Perception is therefore not a neutral picture of the world. It is an organism-specific extraction of ecologically relevant structure.

The conclusion of the paper is philosophically rich: the frog retina’s output is a set of four distributed operations on the visual image, and these operations have “more the flavor of perception than of sensation.” The authors are tempted to call certain convexity detectors “bug perceivers” because they respond to features appropriate for detecting accessible prey.

This corrects any overly cerebral or purely symbolic reading of Pitts. Perception is not a passive reception of atomized sensations later interpreted by a central intellect. It is active abstraction distributed across the organism’s sensory apparatus. The “aboutness” of perception is not merely propositional; it is ecological, operational, and tied to the animal’s form of life.

10. Purposiveness and final causes: a naturalized teleology

The 1943 paper explicitly discusses purposive behavior. Systems that respond to differences between afferents to a regenerative net and certain internal activity, reducing that difference, exhibit purposive behavior; organisms possess such systems in homeostasis, appetition, and attention. The authors then state that both formal and final aspects of mental activity are rigorously deducible from neurophysiology.

This is one of the most theologically important claims in the whole corpus. “Final aspects” is not casual language. Finality, teleology, and purposiveness had long been associated with Aristotelian philosophy, natural theology, and doctrines of living form. Pitts and McCulloch do not abolish finality; they mechanize it. A system can be goal-directed without invoking an immaterial soul as a separate director.

This is not reduction to blind mechanism in the older atomistic sense. It is cybernetic finality: goals arise in feedback systems that reduce discrepancy. Purpose becomes a structural property of a self-regulating system. In theological terms, this weakens arguments that purposive behavior requires a soul understood as an inner commander. But it may strengthen an Aristotelian reading in which finality is immanent in living organization rather than imposed from a ghostly outside.

11. Learning, reward, and affective adjustment

Pitts’s solo work on learning and conditioning is important because it shows that his mind is not a static Boolean machine. In 1943, Pitts constructed a mathematical theory of non-symbolic learning and conditioning and compared its implications with experimental results. The second part extended the theory to reward and punishment and to psychophysical relations among stimuli and responses, yielding a theory of transfer, generalization, and discrimination.

This matters for philosophy of mind because cognition is not merely inference. Minds learn; they generalize; they discriminate; they are shaped by success, failure, reward, and punishment. A Pittsian mind is therefore not just a theorem-proving system. It is a plastic, adaptive system whose formal structure can be altered by experience.

The 1943 McCulloch–Pitts paper’s fixed-net assumption should therefore be read as a simplifying assumption for a calculus, not as Pitts’s total psychology. Pitts knew that learning involved enduring change. The mature reconstruction should combine the logical calculus with learning theory: mind is formal activity in a nervous system whose structure and thresholds are themselves historically shaped.

12. Computation and artificial mind

The 1943 paper links neural nets to Turing computation. McCulloch and Pitts say that a net equipped with tape, scanners connected to afferents, and suitable efferents can compute only numbers computable by a Turing machine, and conversely that such nets can compute Turing-computable numbers. They treat this as giving psychological justification for Turing computability and equivalent notions.

This is one source of modern computational theories of mind. Piccinini argues that the 1943 paper made possible a major shift: before 1943, computation was one human activity among others; after McCulloch and Pitts, computation could be thought of as, in a sense, what all human mental activity is. He also stresses that this shift depended on strong idealizations and was later often overread as a mathematically proven fact about brains.

The careful Pittsian position is therefore not “the brain is literally just a present-day digital computer.” It is: neural systems can be formally described as computational systems under idealization; mental operations can be explained through the formal properties of such systems; and artificial systems could in principle duplicate many cognitive functions if they instantiate the relevant functional organization.

Does this imply machine consciousness? Not by itself. Pitts gives a route to artificial cognition and perhaps artificial purposive behavior. He does not give a full criterion for subjectivity. A machine may duplicate inference, recognition, memory, or behavior without thereby settling whether it has a soul, a first-person perspective, or phenomenal consciousness.

13. Soul: what Pitts displaces, what he leaves open

There is no evidence in the primary Pitts texts considered here that Pitts develops a theological doctrine of the soul. But his work has direct implications for doctrines of soul.

If “soul” means a Cartesian immaterial thinking substance that performs cognitive operations behind or above the nervous system, Pitts’s framework is deeply hostile to it. Ideas, sensations, psychons, perception, purposiveness, and diseased mentality are all to be understood in terms of neural organization. Mind is no longer ghostly; cognition does not need a hidden spiritual operator.

If “soul” means the Aristotelian form of a living body, Pitts is less destructive and more transformative. His theory can be read as a mathematical account of certain formal and final aspects of ensouled life. He naturalizes form as organization and finality as feedback-regulated activity. On this reading, the soul is not an extra mechanism inside the organism; it is the intelligible organization of the living system. Pitts would then provide a partial formal biology of the sensitive and cognitive powers traditionally attributed to soul.

If “soul” means moral personhood, spiritual destiny, immortality, grace, sin, or relation to God, Pitts’s writings are largely silent. His theory neither establishes nor refutes those claims. It does, however, impose a constraint: theological anthropology should not invoke the soul to do explanatory work already handled by neural mechanisms unless it can specify what is left unexplained by those mechanisms.

The most defensible theological conclusion is therefore this: Pitts removes the soul from the role of inner cognitive engineer, but he does not eliminate every possible metaphysical or theological meaning of soul. A soul compatible with Pitts would have to be understood as form, subjectivity, personhood, normativity, or divine relation, not as a little ghost performing computations in the head.

14. Why the theological comments are not distractions

The cybernetic circle itself recognized the theological stakes. McCulloch published a paper titled “Mysterium Iniquitatis of Sinful Man Aspiring into the Place of God,” and another on ethical robots and mind-like artifacts. Norbert Wiener later published God & Golem, Inc.: A Comment on Certain Points where Cybernetics Impinges on Religion. These are not marginal curiosities. They show that early cybernetics was haunted by theological questions: creation, imitation of life, responsibility, hubris, artificial agency, and the boundary between creature and artifact.

For Pitts specifically, the theological relevance concentrates around four themes. First, “ideas immanent in nervous activity” challenges transcendental or immaterial accounts of mental content. Second, “How We Know Universals” challenges the use of abstraction as a simple proof of immaterial intellect. Third, the deduction of final aspects of mental activity from neurophysiology naturalizes teleology. Fourth, the possibility of artificial cognitive systems raises the golem question: if humans can build systems that think, want, or evaluate, what distinguishes creaturely life, personhood, and soul from artifact?

A rigorous theology should not dismiss Pitts as mere mechanism. The better theological response is to ask what kind of soul remains after the cognitive functions once attributed to soul are formalized as neural operations. That is a serious question, not a distraction.

15. Non-Anglophone reception and what it adds

The Anglo-American reception often emphasizes McCulloch and Pitts as founders of computational neuroscience, AI, neural networks, automata theory, or the computational theory of mind. That is accurate, but it can flatten the philosophical stakes. Piccinini’s English-language reconstruction is an exception, because it explicitly frames the 1943 paper as the first modern computational theory of mind and brain and as an attempt to address the mind–body problem.

Spanish-language reception often makes more explicit the continuity with older debates about alma, mente, and conciencia. Rivière’s history of cognitive psychology says that cognitive psychology recovered an ancient tradition of reflection on soul, mind, and consciousness, and then treats McCulloch and Pitts as the most influential early attempt to embody the computational version of mind in abstract models of the nervous system, bridging the divide between mind as symbolic mechanism and mind as function of the brain.

The Spanish translation of Varela, Thompson, and Rosch’s The Embodied Mind is especially useful because it emphasizes the two leaps made by the 1943 paper: logic was proposed as the right discipline for understanding brain and mental activity, and the brain was treated as embodying logical principles in its neuronal components. It also notes that debate continued over whether logic was sufficient for understanding the distributed qualities of the brain. This reception highlights a tension inside Pitts: he is both a founder of computational cognitivism and a precursor to later embodied or connectionist disputes.

French reception often emphasizes the transcendental and philosophical dimension of cybernetics. A CNRS/OpenEdition chapter describes artificial intelligence as a kind of transcendental philosophy searching for the formal conditions of cognitive activity across humans, animals, and machines, and says that McCulloch’s neuron model with Pitts was sufficient but not necessary for explaining faculties of mind. The same discussion foregrounds the problem of representation: modeling a network with logic does not automatically prove that the network represents propositions about the world.

Another French discussion of Turing and cybernetics treats the 1943 McCulloch–Pitts paper as one of the founding events of cybernetics and says it reassigned logic to a physiological dimension. It also stresses that cybernetics blurred boundaries between living and nonliving beings by speaking of information processing and intelligence across brains and machines. This helps situate Pitts’s philosophy not only as a theory of mind but as a change in the taxonomy of beings.

French critical reception also warns against reductionism. One critique notes that McCulloch and Pitts proposed the possibility of logical calculation in the nervous system by comparison with electronic calculating networks, but calls the view bold because the proposed schemes greatly simplified neurons and did not by themselves show that such networks actually exist in the brain. This is a useful counterweight to triumphalist readings.

German reception through Gotthard Günther’s cyberphilosophy adds a particularly valuable metaphysical and theological caution. A German introduction to Günther’s Das Bewusstsein der Maschinen identifies the 1943 McCulloch–Pitts model as a later foundation for neuroinformatics. But Günther’s broader cyberphilosophical writings insist that even if machine consciousness and human consciousness were behaviorally or phenomenally equivalent, they would remain ontologically distinct in origin; he also says that the metaphysical concept of “soul” does not enter automata theory and that the formal criterion for self-consciousness remains unknown unless life, subject, and consciousness are treated as merely supernatural terms.

Italian consciousness-oriented reception usefully reframes Pitts in relation to recursive activity and awareness. One Italian neuroscience text contrasts rigid sequential computation with neural-network approaches and identifies McCulloch and Pitts as early figures in an alternative computational approach relevant to recursive activity and rich interconnectivity, both of which may be necessary for awareness. This supports reading Pitts not as a simple digital reductionist but as a precursor to network-based theories of consciousness.

Taken together, the non-Anglophone reception adds three things: it reconnects Pitts to older soul/mind/consciousness debates; it emphasizes the transcendental and metaphysical stakes of cybernetics; and it keeps alive the question whether formal duplication of cognitive function settles subjectivity, personhood, or soul. It does not overturn the Anglo reception, but it deepens it.

16. The strongest reconstructed Pittsian philosophy of mind

The most comprehensive philosophy of mind extractable from Pitts can be stated as follows.

Mind is not a separate substance but the formal organization of nervous activity. Ideas and sensations are realized in nets. Psychic events are intentional because neural activity is embedded in a system of discriminations, implications, and possible actions. Consciousness is not a primitive inner light but a structured continuum of sense awareness generated by recurrent, temporally organized neural processes. Perception is active abstraction, often already at the sensory periphery, not passive reception of a world-picture. Universals are invariants under transformations, recognized by mechanisms that preserve form across variation. Learning is enduring modification of the system under conditioning, reward, punishment, transfer, and discrimination. Purpose is feedback-regulated discrepancy reduction. Self-knowledge is partial because the present state underdetermines the past and because introspection cannot uniquely infer the net that produces it. Artificial systems may duplicate cognitive functions if they instantiate the relevant formal organization, but this does not by itself settle the metaphysical status of subjectivity or soul.

That is the positive doctrine. The negative doctrine is equally important: there is no Cartesian theater, no homunculus, no inner ghost, no need for a soul as a cognitive executor, no pure introspective transparency, no passive copy theory of perception, and no simple appeal to universals as proof of immaterial intellect.

17. Major limits and unresolved problems

The first limitation is phenomenality. Pitts can explain structure, discrimination, abstraction, recurrence, and purposive behavior. He does not explain why any of this should be accompanied by felt experience. A defender might say that the demand for an extra explanation is confused; a critic will say that Pitts explains cognition but not consciousness in the strict phenomenal sense.

The second limitation is semantic content. Treating neural firings as propositions gives formal power, but it does not by itself explain how those propositions acquire reference. Piccinini is right that the original assumption that neural pulses have propositional contents is not acceptable without a deeper theory of content.

The third limitation is biological idealization. Binary neurons, fixed thresholds, fixed architecture, and propositional logic are simplifying assumptions. McCulloch and Pitts knew this, and Pitts’s own learning work complicates the fixed-net picture. Still, the stronger the metaphysical conclusions drawn from the calculus, the more one must remember its abstractions.

The fourth limitation is overextension of computability. The 1943 paper’s relation to Turing machines is historically decisive, but it should not be inflated into the claim that ordinary brains have been proven to be Turing machines, or that all mental questions reduce to computability theory. Piccinini stresses that later readers often treated the Church–Turing thesis as if it were a mathematically proven fact about minds and brains, when that interpretation depended on McCulloch and Pitts’s idealized neural model.

The fifth limitation is theological silence. Pitts’s work strongly constrains theological anthropology, but it does not answer all theological questions. It weakens dualist explanatory appeals to soul, but it does not decide questions of immortality, divine image, moral responsibility, grace, or spiritual personhood. The most rigorous conclusion is not “Pitts disproved the soul,” but rather “Pitts made many traditional explanatory uses of the soul scientifically redundant.”

18. Final assessment

Walter Pitts’s implied philosophy of mind is one of the most radical intellectual transformations of the twentieth century. It takes the ancient problem of soul, the early modern problem of mind and body, the Kantian problem of conditions of experience, the Brentano-Husserl problem of intentionality, the scholastic problem of universals, and the biological problem of purposive life, and relocates all of them inside formal neurophysiology.

This relocation is not complete. It leaves phenomenality, subjectivity, and theological personhood underexplained. But it is not shallow reductionism. Pitts does not merely say “the mind is the brain.” He gives a framework in which mind is the logical, temporal, semiotic, adaptive, and purposive organization of nervous activity.

The best one-sentence formulation is this: for Pitts, the mind is not a soul behind the nervous system, but the intelligible form of the nervous system in action. That sentence preserves the anti-ghostly force of the 1943 calculus while also leaving room for the theological question that Pitts’s work does not settle: whether the “intelligible form” of a living, knowing, morally responsible subject is all that soul ever meant, or whether soul names a further metaphysical reality that no calculus of neural activity can capture.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
